sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 38, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 37 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.3x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Excellent! After moving LEFT:
- The right charcoal box now has 16 pixels (repaired!) - boundary=[[26, 44], [26, 48], [30, 48], [30, 44]]
- The right green box moved from (30-32, 42-44) to (30-32, 39-41) - it moved LEFT 3 columns
- The left charcoal box still has 15 pixels (damaged)
- The left green box stayed at (30-32, 9-11)
So the LEFT action moved the RIGHT green box, not the left one! This confirms that the "active" green box is the rightmost one (or the one closest to the center).
The right charcoal box is now repaired (16 pixels). The left charcoal box is still damaged (15 pixels).
Now I need to repair the left charcoal box. The left green box at (30-32, 9-11) overlaps with the left charcoal box at (32-36, 11-15) at position (32, 11).
To repair the left charcoal box, I need to move the left green box away. But the active green box is the right one. How do I switch to the left green box?
Looking at the pattern:
- When I moved RIGHT, the left green box moved (it was the active one)
- When I moved LEFT, the right green box moved (it was the active one)
So the active green box seems to be the one that moves in the direction of the action. If I move LEFT, the right green box moves LEFT. If I move RIGHT, the left green box moves RIGHT.
Wait, that doesn't make sense. Let me re-examine.
Actually, looking at the history:
1. Initially, there was one green box at (30-32, 12-14) with a white dot
2. After moving RIGHT 6 times, it moved to (30-32, 42-44) and a new green box appeared at (30-32, 27-29)
3. After moving LEFT 6 times, the green box at (30-32, 27-29) moved to (30-32, 9-11)
4. After moving LEFT 1 time, the green box at (30-32, 42-44) moved to (30-32, 39-41)
So it seems like:
- RIGHT moves the leftmost green box
- LEFT moves the rightmost green box
This makes sense! LEFT moves the rightmost green box LEFT, and RIGHT moves the leftmost green box RIGHT.
So to move the left green box (at 30-32, 9-11), I need to use RIGHT. But RIGHT would move it towards the charcoal box, not away.
Wait, the left green box is at (30-32, 9-11) and the left charcoal box is at (32-36, 11-15). The overlap is at (32, 11). To move the green box away from the charcoal box, I need to move it LEFT (to col 6-8) or UP (to row 27-29) or DOWN (to row 33-35).
But LEFT moves the rightmost green box, not the leftmost. And RIGHT moves the leftmost green box, but that would move it towards the charcoal box.
Hmm, maybe I need to move the right green box out of the way first, then move the left green box.
Actually wait, let me re-read the current state:
- Left green box at (30-32, 9-11)
- Right green box at (30-32, 39-41)
- Left charcoal box at (32-36, 11-15) with 15 pixels (damaged at 32, 11)
- Right charcoal box at (26-30, 44-48) with 16 pixels (repaired)
The left green box overlaps with the left charcoal box at (32, 11). To fix this, I need to move the left green box away.
Since RIGHT moves the leftmost green box, and the left green box is at (30-32, 9-11), moving RIGHT would move it to (30-32, 10-12) or (30-32, 12-14), which would make the overlap worse.
But maybe the game logic is different. Let me try moving DOWN to see if the left green box can move DOWN, clearing the overlap at row 32.
Actually, let me think about this differently. The left green box is at (30-32, 9-11). The charcoal box is at (32-36, 11-15). The overlap is at row 32, col 11. If I move the green box DOWN, it would go to (31-33, 9-11), and the overlap would be at rows 32-33, cols 11. That's worse.
If I move the green box UP, it would go to (29-31, 9-11), and there would be no overlap with the charcoal box at (32-36, 11-15). That would fix the charcoal box!
But how do I move the left green box UP? Let me try UP and see which green box moves.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# GREAT FINDING! LEFT moved the right green box, repairing the right charcoal box (16 pixels)!
# Right charcoal box: 16 pixels (repaired)
# Left charcoal box: 15 pixels (still damaged by left green box)
#
# Movement rule: LEFT moves rightmost green box, RIGHT moves leftmost green box
# Need to move left green box (30-32, 9-11) away from left charcoal box (32-36, 11-15)
# Overlap at (32, 11)
#
# Try UP to move the left green box up, clearing the overlap
result = action(['UP'])
print(f"After UP: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")

seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] >= 10:
        print(f"Charcoal box: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"Green box: boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

Green box: boundary=[[30, 9], [30, 11], [32, 11], [32, 9]]
